does: This paper presents WundtGPT, a fine-tuned large language model designed to function as an empathetic and proactive mental health assistant. The model is trained using instruction tuning and real psychologist-patient conversation data to embody two key attributes: empathy and proactive guidance. WundtGPT goes beyond providing generic psychological suggestions by asking purposeful, symptom-eliciting questions and offering warm emotional reassurance. The system incorporates a Collection of Questions mechanism to guide patient interactions systematically, aiming to help psychologists with diagnosis while also supporting patients reluctant to engage in face-to-face therapy by fostering an authentic doctor-patient relationship through natural conversation.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research applying large language models to healthcare and mental health domains, extending beyond static medical knowledge retrieval toward relational and emotional competencies. It addresses limitations in existing mental health LLMs that provide reasonable suggestions but lack the interpersonal dimensions essential to clinical effectiveness. The research draws on classical psychology theory, engaging with Wundt's foundational work on empathy and introspection, while synthesizing recent advances in instruction tuning, conversational AI, and fine-tuning approaches demonstrated across medical LLMs. It responds to documented gaps in how LLMs can authentically simulate the dynamic, emotionally-informed interactions that characterize effective therapeutic relationships.
enables: This work opens pathways for developing AI systems that can serve as scalable mental health supports and diagnostic aids, particularly for populations facing barriers to traditional therapy. Future work could extend these techniques to other medical specialties requiring relationship-building and emotional attunement. The framework of decomposing clinical relationships into learnable attributes like empathy and proactivity could inform development of more sophisticated clinical LLMs across psychiatry, counseling, and palliative care. Additionally, the approach raises research directions in measuring and validating emotional authenticity in AI systems, understanding when patients form productive therapeutic alliances with AI versus human clinicians, and integrating such systems into hybrid care models where machines augment rather than replace human providers.
